title: Building and using a root cellar for long-term food storage
subtitle: Creating stable cool-and-humid storage conditions for root vegetables, apples, and preserves without refrigeration
excerpt: A root cellar maintains 1 to 4 degrees Celsius and 85 to 95 percent relative humidity year-round using only earth insulation and natural ventilation, consuming zero electricity. This range preserves potatoes for 4 to 6 months, carrots for 4 to 5 months, and apples for 3 to 4 months with minimal quality loss. The key design parameters are thermal mass, humidity control, and ventilation to remove ethylene gas and excess moisture.
sourceNotes: Root Cellaring: Natural Cold Storage of Fruits and Vegetables (Bubel, 1991). USDA National Institute of Food and Agriculture storage temperature and humidity data. UK Met Office ground temperature data for storage design. Garden Organic crop storage guidance 2024.

glossaryTerms.definition: The ability of a material to absorb and store heat energy. In a root cellar, the surrounding earth provides enormous thermal mass that buffers the internal temperature against surface temperature swings. At 1.5 m below the surface in the UK, ground temperature remains at 8 to 12 degrees Celsius year-round. At 2.5 m depth, ground temperature is approximately 5 to 8 degrees Celsius.

A plant hormone gas released by ripening fruit, especially apples, pears, and tomatoes, that accelerates ripening and causes premature sprouting in root vegetables stored nearby. A single bushel of apples releases enough ethylene to cause potatoes stored in the same enclosed space to sprout within 2 to 4 weeks. Ventilation and physical separation of ethylene producers from root vegetables is essential.

A pre-storage conditioning process for certain root crops (potatoes, sweet potatoes, winter squash) that allows the skin to harden and minor surface damage to heal before long-term storage. Potatoes should be cured at 10 to 15 degrees Celsius at 90 to 95 percent relative humidity for 10 to 14 days before moving to root cellar conditions. Uncured potatoes with damaged skins rot significantly faster in storage.

A passive ventilation strategy using two vents placed at different heights to drive airflow by temperature differential. Cold outside air enters through a low inlet vent and warm, humid air exits through a high outlet vent. The height difference drives the stack effect: even in calm conditions, a 1 m height difference between inlet and outlet provides measurable airflow when the temperature differential is more than 2 degrees Celsius.

content.text: The earth’s
thermal mass

keeps underground temperatures stable throughout the year. Below 1 m in the UK, ground temperature stays between 4 and 15 degrees Celsius regardless of the season above. A root cellar uses this stable zone to store food with no mechanical refrigeration. The key challenges are controlling humidity, limiting
ethylene gas
, and ensuring the space stays cold enough for temperature-sensitive crops.

A traditional above-ground straw-insulated clamp is a lower-cost alternative to a built root cellar for potato and root vegetable storage in climates where ground temperature is sufficient. Clamps require only straw and soil and cost almost nothing. They are less suitable for apples (ethylene builds up), preserves (temperature swings), or extended storage beyond March. A root cellar is the right solution where the household produces significant volumes of multiple storage crops or needs year-round access.
Design principles
Depth: the floor should be at least 1.8 m below surface level to access stable ground temperature. A 2.0 to 2.5 m depth is preferable in the UK Midlands and North. Excavate manually (using a
spade
and bucket) or hire a mini-excavator for larger cellars.
Ventilation: install two vents of 100 to 150 mm diameter using
differential ventilation
. The low inlet vent near floor level draws cold air in; the high outlet vent near ceiling level expels warm, humid air and
ethylene gas
. Both vents must have insect-proof mesh and adjustable dampers to control ventilation rate in cold snaps when the external temperature drops below zero.

Humidity: an earth floor maintains humidity by allowing moisture to evaporate from the soil. If the floor is concrete, keep open trays of sand or water on the floor to maintain 85 to 95 percent relative humidity. A humidity of below 70 percent causes root vegetables to lose moisture and become flaccid within 4 to 8 weeks.

Construction: a simple root cellar can be constructed from 4-inch concrete block, poured concrete, or treated timber framing with fibre cement cladding set into a hillside. Locate against a north-facing slope or shaded bank where possible to minimise summer solar heating of the earth above the cellar. A 2 m × 2 m internal floor area stores enough produce for a household of 4 through winter.
What to store and how
Potatoes: cure for 10 to 14 days at 10 to 15 degrees Celsius before storage.
Cured
potatoes store at 2 to 4 degrees Celsius for 4 to 6 months. Store in hessian sacks or wooden slatted crates, not polythene bags. Exclude all light to prevent greening (solanine development).

Carrots and parsnips: pack in slightly moist (not wet) sand in wooden boxes. Layering in sand prevents moisture loss and cushions against physical damage. Do not allow to touch each other. Store at 1 to 3 degrees Celsius for 4 to 5 months.

Apples: store in a separate section or box from root vegetables to contain ethylene. Wrap each apple in newspaper or store on slatted trays without touching. Check monthly and remove any that show signs of rot. Late-season apple varieties (Bramley, Cox Orange Pippin) store well; early varieties do not.
What done well looks like

A 2 m × 2 m earth-floored cellar built into a north-facing bank maintains 3 to 5 degrees Celsius and 90 percent relative humidity from October through March. 80 kg of Desiree potatoes (cured for 12 days) store until late February without sprouting. 40 kg of carrots in sand boxes are still firm in March. 30 kg of Bramley apples in newspaper-wrapped trays are used steadily through winter with less than 5 percent wastage to rot. The household eliminates winter purchases of stored root crops entirely.
What done badly looks like

Potatoes are stored uncured and in polythene bags. Moisture condenses inside the bags and 30 percent of the crop rots by December. Apples are stored in the same enclosed space as potatoes without ventilation to clear ethylene; the potatoes sprout aggressively within 3 weeks. The cellar has no ventilation and humidity drops to 55 percent because the floor is sealed concrete with no moisture source; carrots stored in open trays without sand are limp and dried by January.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Temperature inside the cellar rises above 8 degrees Celsius in late summer
attrs.cause: Depth is insufficient (less than 1.5 m to floor), the cellar is on a south-facing slope receiving direct solar gain, or the access door is uninsulated and transfers heat
attrs.fix: Insulate the access door with 50 mm of rigid foam board. If the cellar is shallow, additional earth covering (a 300 to 500 mm bund of soil over the roof) adds thermal mass and delays the seasonal temperature swing. Delay stocking the cellar until external temperatures drop below 10 degrees Celsius in autumn to avoid warming the thermal mass before storage begins.

attrs.symptom: Potatoes sprout despite being stored at the correct temperature
attrs.cause: Apples or other ethylene-producing produce is stored in the same space without adequate ventilation to clear the gas
attrs.fix: Physically separate ethylene producers from root vegetables using a dividing wall or a separate lidded container. Increase ventilation rate to dilute ethylene gas: open the high outlet vent fully. Check whether any produce stored nearby is overripe and producing elevated ethylene as it breaks down.

attrs.symptom: Carrots become soft and limp within 6 weeks of storage
attrs.cause: Humidity is too low, causing the carrots to lose moisture through transpiration
attrs.fix: Pack carrots in damp (not wet) sand in solid-sided wooden boxes with lids. The sand should feel cool and barely moist when squeezed; 10 to 15 percent moisture content by weight. Measure relative humidity with a cheap digital hygrometer: if below 80 percent, add open trays of damp sand or buckets of water to the cellar floor space.
